summary: The 997 is generally well-built, but the 997.1 has a serious IMS bearing concern that can destroy the engine. The 997.2 with DFI engine largely resolved this issue.

issues.name: IMS Bearing Failure (997.1)
issues.description: The intermediate shaft bearing in the 997.1 Carrera can fail catastrophically, destroying the engine. Affects 997.1 (2005–2008) but not 997.2 models.

issues.name: Bore Scoring (3.8L)
issues.description: The 3.8L engine in Carrera S models can develop bore scoring — scratches in the cylinder walls caused by failed Lokasil coating or debris.

issues.name: Coolant Pipe Leaks
issues.description: The plastic coolant pipes in the rear of the engine develop leaks over time. Located in a hard-to-reach area above the transmission.

issues.name: PASM Shock Absorber Failure
issues.description: The Porsche Active Suspension Management (PASM) electronic dampers can leak or fail, requiring expensive replacement at approximately $1,000 per corner.

issues.name: RMS (Rear Main Seal) Oil Leak
issues.description: The rear main seal between the engine and transmission develops an oil leak. Requires transmission removal to replace — labor intensive.

buyingTips: 997.1 vs 997.2 — the 997.2 (2009+) eliminated the IMS bearing concern with a new DFI engine. Strongly prefer 997.2 if IMS worries you
Request a bore scope inspection before purchase — especially on 3.8L Carrera S models to check for bore scoring
Verify IMS bearing has been upgraded on 997.1 cars — LN Engineering single-row retrofit is the gold standard
Check for oil leaks at the RMS — look under the engine for oil dripping from the engine/transmission junction
Inspect PASM operation — drive over bumps in Comfort and Sport modes to verify the dampers adjust properly
